title: Wallets, keys, and self-custody
summary: HD wallets, seed phrases, descriptors, hardware signers, multisig, inheritance planning. The mechanics behind self-custody, not vendor recommendations.

edges.label: HD wallets and BIP32 derivation
edges.summary: How a single seed produces an unbounded tree of keys, why that lets a wallet hand out fresh addresses without backing anything up, and what the derivation path on every wallet screen actually means.

edges.label: Seed phrases (BIP39)
edges.summary: Twelve or twenty-four words encode the entropy your whole wallet derives from. The wordlist, the optional passphrase, and the failure modes worth knowing before you write anything down on paper.

edges.label: Output descriptors
edges.summary: The format that finally lets wallets describe their own watch-only setup unambiguously — script type, derivation path, key origin — instead of every wallet inventing its own export blob.

edges.label: Hardware signers and the air-gap
edges.summary: Why moving the signing key off the internet-connected machine is the single biggest improvement to a custody setup, what an air-gapped signer actually defends against, and the open-source vs proprietary question.

edges.label: Multisig and key separation
edges.summary: k-of-n setups remove single-point-of-failure for both theft and loss. The sane configurations, the geographic and vendor diversity that actually matter, and the operational complexity tax.

edges.label: Inheritance and recovery planning
edges.summary: How to make sure your coins are recoverable by your heirs without making them stealable by your enemies. The two main design patterns and the legal-versus-cryptographic tradeoffs.
